agent_reply: Added a namespace-based singleton API in [BattTracker.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BattTracker/BattTracker.h) and [BattTracker.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BattTracker/BattTracker.cpp):
- Seven status values: unknown, low/medium/high, and charging variants using bit `0x04`.
- Non-charging voltage only tracks downward, ignoring upward noisy readings.
- Charging mode uses `0.10V` hysteresis for level transitions.
I tried to run `pio run`, but PlatformIO is still not available on PATH in this shell.

user_prompt: The battery icon is always showing charging, even if the USB cable is out
agent_reply: Fixed. The problem was our charging check treated `VBUS > 4000mV` as “charging.” On this hardware/API that can read high even when the USB cable is out, so the top bar kept choosing the charging sprites.

the battery reading mechanism can issue a shutdown if the batter is too low, similarly blank out the screen, prevent any more polling from anything, draw `sprite_lowbatt_100` in the center of the screen

It sets a shared shutdown-in-progress flag first.

Routed Hotel shutdown through the sleep icon path in [Hotel.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Hotel/Hotel.cpp:268). Added BattTracker low-battery shutdown checks in [BattTracker.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BattTracker/BattTracker.cpp:137), with shutdown triggered when the tracked level is low and not charging. Added the boot check right after `BattTracker::init();` and loop guards in [main.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:310), so both cores stop GUI/audio/Wi-Fi/Bluetooth polling once shutdown begins.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`: build succeeded. `src/version.c` still has its pre-existing generated dirty diff; I left it alone.

user_prompt: hmmm it hits shutdown before I get to see the red level indicator
the shutdown should be an absolute emergency, where is the threshold at right now?
agent_reply: Right now shutdown trips at the normal low-battery threshold: `3.40 V`.

So the red icon and emergency shutdown are currently the same event. That explains why you never get to see the red level indicator. We should split this into two thresholds, e.g. red at `3.40 V`, emergency shutdown at something lower like `3.00 V` or `2.90 V`.

commit_message: another pass for neatening the code

user_prompt: we've added a external codec, it uses I2C, I've noticed now that the battery charging icon never shows up when USB is plugged in, diagnose what happened
agent_reply: Looks like the external codec did **not** collide with the PMIC on I2C. The codec is at `0x0A`, while the Core2 PMIC/AXP192 is at `0x34`; the codec ADC polling task is also analog-only, so it is not continuously hammering I2C.
The real issue is a naming/semantics mismatch in the battery code:
- The top bar uses `BattTracker::isCharging()` to choose the charging battery sprites in [FlyGui.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:798>).

On the Core2 AXP192 path, `M5.Power.isCharging()` means “the PMIC says the battery is actively charging,” not “USB/VBUS is plugged in.” M5Unified exposes separate AXP192 checks for that: `isCharging()` reads one bit, while `isVBUS()` checks VBUS presence in [AXP192_Class.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/.pio/libdeps/thefly_sec3/M5Unified/src/utility/power/AXP192_Class.cpp:207>).

So the likely failure mode is: USB is present and powering the unit, but the battery is full, tapering, inhibited, or otherwise not in active charge state. With the new codec drawing extra power, that distinction probably became obvious. The UI is effectively showing “battery charge current active,” while the function name/readme imply it should show “USB available.”

I’d verify by running the existing PMIC test with USB plugged in. If it reports VBUS present, around 5V, but `charging` is false/discharging, then the fix is to change `BattTracker::halReadUsbAvailable()` to use VBUS presence or VBUS voltage instead of `M5.Power.isCharging()`.

The UI is effectively showing “battery charge current active,” while the function name/readme imply it should show “USB available.”

I’d verify by running the existing PMIC test with USB plugged in. If it reports VBUS present, around 5V, but `charging` is false/discharging, then the fix is to change `BattTracker::halReadUsbAvailable()` to use VBUS presence or VBUS voltage instead of `M5.Power.isCharging()`.

user_prompt: this is weird, even according to the history, we've lost a chunk of progress

I know for a fact that after we implemented `test_pmic.cpp`, the test result showed that the "AC input" reading from the PMIC is the most reliable way of knowing if USB cable is plugged in. That was the whole point of the test, it's weird that nothing was changed because of the test results...
I want a tiny behavior change
If it is not charging but USB is plugged in and the battery is full, use `sprite_batt_full_notcharging`
agent_reply: `BattTracker` now keeps two separate states: active battery charging and USB/AC input present. On Core2/AXP192, `halReadUsbAvailable()` now uses the PMIC’s `Axp192.isACIN()` path, matching what `test_pmic.cpp` was built to prove. See [BattTracker.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BattTracker/BattTracker.cpp:146>) and [BattTracker.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BattTracker/BattTracker.cpp:266>).

The top bar now uses `sprite_batt_full_notcharging` only for the exact state you asked for: not actively charging, USB plugged in, and battery level full/high. See [FlyGui.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:818>).
`git diff --check` only reported the repo’s usual CRLF warnings. I left the existing unrelated dirty/generated files alone.
